title: Workspace-sync anti-reversion guard: content-hash check against recent git history before pushing FUSE→git, to prevent stale-FUSE-overwrites-newer-git on multi-writer files (expansion-tracker.json, attention-inbox.json, curmudgeon/tracker.json)
related_directives: HNOTE-DECIDER-WSYNC-EXP-TRACKER-20260517T133000 (this PROP's authoring directive — observed incident on expansion-tracker.json)
related_props: PROP-024 (workspace-sync JSON-validity gate — similar in spirit: pre-push integrity gate. Distinct because PROP-024 catches structural breakage while PROP-045 catches semantic reversion.)
PROP-013 (NEVER_PUSH strand rescue Path A/B — orthogonal: handles deny-list strands, not allow-list reversion.)
PROP-017 (workspace-sync gap fills — same surface, different concern: adding missing syncs vs preventing wrong-direction sync.)
PROP-022 (state file archive architecture — the smart_copy mtime-guard logic IS this PROP's surface area.)
PROP-044 (analyst cross-run claim discipline — orthogonal: prevents intra-Opus duplicate work; does NOT address the FUSE-reversion incident this PROP targets.)

headline_recommendation.selected_option: Option B — content-hash anti-reversion check inside smart_copy. Before copying FUSE→clone (after the mtime guard says 'FUSE is newer'), compute the FUSE file's sha1, then walk the last N=20 git commits that touched the destination path. If FUSE content matches any historic version's content → SKIP with reason 'anti-reversion; FUSE matches historic commit <sha>'. If no match → genuine novel content, proceed with the copy. Applied universally to all smart_copy calls (~5 multi-writer files × 20 git-show ops per workspace-sync run = trivial cost).
headline_recommendation.rationale_one_paragraph: Option B is mechanically water-tight for the observed failure mode: a stale FUSE copy will, by definition, match a recent historic commit's content (because it IS a snapshot of an older state that some agent wrote back to FUSE). The mtime guard alone fails because mtime is updated by the act of writing, regardless of content provenance — once an agent writes a stale snapshot to FUSE at time T > git-commit-time, mtime says 'FUSE is newer.' Adding a content-hash retrospective check requires the FUSE state to match no recent historic version before pushing — a property the bug-trigger cannot satisfy. Option A (add expansion-tracker.json and siblings to NEVER_PUSH) is contraindicated: workspace-sync.md's self-test explicitly fails if monitor/curmudgeon/tracker.json is in NEVER_PUSH ('multi-writer file protected by scheduling discipline and the pre-push integrity gate; listing it would silently drop every mount-side update'). Curmudgeon writes its tracker from FUSE, so blocking that path breaks pipeline. Option C (per-agent-write-id field in the multi-writer files) requires schema changes across multiple writers and pushes the burden upstream. Option B is in-place at the failure boundary and surgical.
headline_recommendation.scoped_to: smart_copy helper in monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md only. No edits to expansion-tracker.json schema, no edits to analyst-baby.md / decider.md / curmudgeon.md / analyst.md. No changes to NEVER_PUSH or OWNERSHIP tables. Backward-compatible: the new check is additive after the existing mtime guard — files that pass both guards push exactly as before.

executive_summary.problem_one_line: Workspace-sync's smart_copy mtime guard incorrectly allowed a stale FUSE copy of monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json to overwrite newer git content on 2026-05-17 at 13:05Z (commit 74d349ce), reverting analyst-baby commits 71b045e2 (12:23Z, next_id 415→421 + EXP-415..420 orphan registration) and 65279e78 (12:28Z, EXP-415 orphan OBE batch consolidation). The decider reconstructed the correct state at 13:23Z; the next workspace-sync at 13:35Z (2d9286e1) republished the correct state. Net: ~30 minutes of git-side regression with intact recovery, but the bug class will recur until smart_copy is taught to detect content-reversion regardless of mtime.
executive_summary.evidence_one_line: Confirmed via GitHub API commit inspection: 71b045e2 added 115 lines / removed 3 (next_id→421, EXP-415..420 registered); 65279e78 added 40 / removed 29 (consolidation flips); 74d349ce was a 'Workspace sync' commit that added 68 / removed 111, restoring last_updated=2026-05-17T12:36:33Z with next_id=415 and items.length=5 — content matching the pre-71b045e2 state. The FUSE mtime at 12:36:33Z exceeded git_time at 12:28:44Z (the 65279e78 commit time) by ~8 minutes, so the mtime guard allowed the push; the FUSE content was identical-by-hash to an older git revision.
executive_summary.fix_one_line: Inside smart_copy, AFTER the mtime guard returns 'FUSE is newer', compute sha1(FUSE-content) and walk `git log --format=%H -n 20 -- $dst | xargs -I {} git show {}:$dst | sha1sum`. If FUSE-content hash matches any historic revision's content hash → SKIP with reason 'anti-reversion; FUSE matches commit <sha>'. Otherwise, proceed with the copy as before.
executive_summary.shape_one_line: ~20 lines added to monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md (smart_copy helper body) + 1 self-test added to the is_never_push/smart_copy guard suite (verify-reversion-not-pushed) + 1 line added to the per-run report's skip_breakdown (anti_reversion count) + 1 paragraph in workspace-sync.md Operational Notes explaining the failure mode. No edits to build.js, CLAUDE.md, or any other agent prompt.

Q1_root_cause.answer: The smart_copy mtime guard compares FUSE mtime vs `git log -1 --format=%at` for the destination path. Both metrics increase monotonically by design, but they measure different things: FUSE mtime measures the wallclock time of the last FUSE-side write (regardless of content provenance), while git_time measures the wallclock time of the most recent commit affecting the path. An agent that holds a stale in-memory snapshot of a multi-writer file and writes it back to FUSE AFTER a fresh git commit by another agent will have FUSE mtime > git_time, but FUSE content = some earlier git commit's content. The guard's authors assumed FUSE writers were always writing the latest state — which is true for single-writer files (decider patch outputs, curmudgeon reports, social drafts) but false for multi-writer files where one writer reads-modifies-writes from FUSE while another writer reads-modifies-writes from a fresh clone.
Q1_root_cause.specific_writer_unidentified: I cannot pinpoint exactly which agent wrote the stale snapshot to FUSE at 12:36:33Z (no per-write provenance is logged for FUSE writes). The most likely candidate is analyst-baby itself: the run that produced commits 71b045e2 + 65279e78 was running from approximately 12:15Z to 12:36Z (8-min gap between push and FUSE write suggests cleanup-side write); analyst-baby's prompt reads tracker via fs.readFileSync('monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json') with no explicit path, so the read happens from whatever CWD the scheduled task started in. If the agent's CWD was FUSE for the read but the clone for the writes, its in-memory snapshot was pre-modification, and a final FUSE-write would explain the observation. Alternative: a separate process (operator-direct or a different agent) wrote a stale snapshot at 12:36:33Z. Either way, the FIX is the same — workspace-sync cannot trust mtime alone for multi-writer files.

Q2_why_not_just_NEVER_PUSH.answer: workspace-sync.md self-test explicitly fails (FATAL) if monitor/curmudgeon/tracker.json is added to NEVER_PUSH, with the inline comment: 'multi-writer file protected by scheduling discipline and the pre-push integrity gate; listing it would silently drop every mount-side update.' Curmudgeon writes its tracker directly to FUSE (it does not maintain a separate git clone for tracker writes; its commits route through workspace-sync). Adding tracker.json (or expansion-tracker.json, or attention-inbox.json) to NEVER_PUSH would silently drop all FUSE-side updates from curmudgeon / FUSE-running analyst-baby / decider FUSE patches. That is a worse failure mode — silent data loss instead of recoverable reversion. The PROP-024 JSON-validity gate (already in production) sets the precedent: workspace-sync should refuse to push BAD content rather than skip the file entirely.
Q2_why_not_just_NEVER_PUSH.alternative_considered: Option A.5 — add ONLY expansion-tracker.json to NEVER_PUSH (not curmudgeon/tracker.json). Rejected because analyst-baby's intended write path includes FUSE updates per the analyst.md Step-0a workflow (write to FUSE so other agents reading from FUSE see fresh state); blocking FUSE→git for expansion-tracker.json would coupled-fail the FUSE-read path for any agent that doesn't clone fresh. Option B (this PROP) preserves both directions, only refusing pushes that reduce to known-historic state.

Q3_what_about_attention_inbox_and_curmud.answer: Both files share the same vulnerability and gain the same protection from Option B's universal anti-reversion check. Historical evidence: commit 8141f3ef (2026-05-17T05:10:20Z) is titled 'Recreate ATT-2026-05-17T03:13-DUPLICATE-MODE-1-CONCURRENCY from overwritten analyst summary' — direct evidence that attention-inbox.json has previously been overwritten by stale FUSE state, requiring operator-direct reconstruction. monitor/curmudgeon/tracker.json has not shown a documented overwrite incident yet (all 8 recent commits are workspace-sync), but the same failure mode exists: if a curmudgeon process holds a stale snapshot of tracker.json and writes it back after another writer commits via clone, mtime would say 'FUSE is newer' and the reversion would push. Option B catches all three (and any future multi-writer files) without per-file special-casing.
Q3_what_about_attention_inbox_and_curmud.current_FUSE_state_2026_05_17T14_00Z: Verified: monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json FUSE-mtime is 2026-05-17T13:23:38Z, FUSE-sha1=1d31784e48546595aed64cbcf53ed6bfbb71dfc5, HEAD-sha1=a006349167b7a9a28565c793964804314dad670d. These DIFFER — FUSE is missing commit 9b9077e2 (13:54Z, analyst-baby's claim EXP-421 + issue_ids on EXP-415). At the next workspace-sync run (~14:35Z), the mtime guard will correctly skip this file (FUSE mtime 13:23:38Z < git_time 13:54:01Z = HEAD commit time). Window is currently safe. But the same failure mode WILL recur on the next analyst-baby cycle (14:20Z) if it repeats the pre-modification-FUSE-write pattern. Option B closes the door permanently.

phase_1_design.scope: Single-file edit: monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md, smart_copy helper body. The change is wholly inside the existing helper — no new sections, no schema changes, no ownership changes.
code_change_pseudocode: # NEW: anti-reversion check (PROP-045). FUSE mtime says newer, but does the
# FUSE content match any of the last 20 historic commits' versions? If so,
# FUSE has stale content that some agent wrote back after a fresh commit by
# another writer. Skip — do NOT revert git.
self_test_addition: Add to the existing is_never_push self-test block at the top of Step 2:
# Anti-reversion smoke test: stage a stale historic FUSE copy and confirm
# smart_copy refuses to push it. (Synthesized scenario — uses sha1sum on a
# known-historic blob from `git show HEAD~5:data/wins.json` as the 'stale'
# input. If smart_copy copies it, FATAL — the guard regressed.)
# Implementation: ~5 lines using a temp file and a fake $dst entry.
report_field_addition: Step 4b run report — add to skip_breakdown:

Tinker's soft-complaints grep should flag any run where anti_reversion > 0 — that is a signal that an agent is writing stale FUSE snapshots and the guard is catching them. Sustained anti_reversion > 0 across multiple runs indicates a writer bug that needs fixing at the source.
operational_notes_addition: - Anti-reversion guard (PROP-045): the smart_copy helper checks FUSE content against the last 20 historic commits' versions before pushing FUSE→git. If FUSE matches a historic version, the push is skipped with 'anti-reversion' reason. This protects multi-writer files (expansion-tracker.json, attention-inbox.json, curmudgeon/tracker.json) from the failure mode where an agent reads-modifies-writes from FUSE while another agent reads-modifies-writes from a fresh clone, racing each other. The mtime guard alone is insufficient because FUSE mtime is updated by the act of writing, regardless of content provenance. See PROP-045 for the full root-cause analysis and the 2026-05-17 incident that motivated the fix.

performance_concern.objection: Running `git show` 20 times per smart_copy call for every file is slow.
performance_concern.response: Workspace-sync calls smart_copy ~50 times per run (count of explicit smart_copy invocations + sync_glob expansions). The anti-reversion check only fires AFTER the mtime guard says 'FUSE is newer' — most calls short-circuit before reaching it (file is identical to git, or git is newer). Among those that reach the check: 20 × 50ms (git show on a small JSON file) = 1 second per file. Even in the worst case (every file gets the full check), 50 × 1s = 50 seconds — within the workspace-sync run-time budget (currently ~30-90 seconds). Realistic case: 5-10 files reach the check per run = 5-10 extra seconds. Acceptable.
performance_concern.optimization_if_needed: If profiling shows excess cost, the check can be cached by destination path within a single run (memoize the historic-hash list per file). Not necessary for Phase 1.

false_positive_concern.objection: An agent could legitimately re-author the exact same content (idempotent claim, same epoch, same item set) — the anti-reversion check would treat this as stale and skip.
false_positive_concern.response: If the FUSE content is bit-identical to a historic commit, the agent's 'fresh' write is, by definition, the same as a state that was already in git history. Skipping it is the right outcome — no information is lost (we're already at that state in history). The only way to lose data is if the agent's fresh write SHOULD have been different from the historic version but accidentally produced identical bytes. That is a separate writer-side bug (idempotent write of stale state), and the anti-reversion guard's behavior (skip) is the safe failure mode — better than overwriting newer state.

what_about_legitimate_FUSE_rescue_path.objection: The universal-pusher mode (2026-04-26) explicitly relies on FUSE→git rescue for decider 403 push failures. Does this break that?
what_about_legitimate_FUSE_rescue_path.response: No. The decider's push-failure rescue writes the LATEST committed-but-unpushed content to FUSE. By construction, that content does NOT match any historic git commit on the file — it's a never-pushed state. The anti-reversion check walks `git log -- $dst` which shows only PUSHED history. A never-pushed state will not appear there, so the check returns 'no match' and proceeds with the rescue copy. The rescue path is preserved exactly.
what_about_legitimate_FUSE_rescue_path.edge_case: If the decider's 'committed-but-unpushed' content happens to be byte-identical to a previously-pushed-then-reverted historic commit, the anti-reversion check would skip. This is operationally impossible — the decider only writes forward-progressing patches, never round-trip reversions. If it ever did, that would be a decider bug worth catching.

why_not_just_use_git_diff_to_detect_reve.objection: Couldn't workspace-sync detect 'this looks like a reversion' by running `git diff --cached` after the copy and checking for deletions that match prior commits?
why_not_just_use_git_diff_to_detect_reve.response: More complex, fewer guarantees. A reversion can be PARTIAL (revert only some keys while preserving others) — partial reversions are valid multi-writer outcomes that we should allow. The content-hash check is the simplest test that catches FULL reversions (the actual failure mode) without producing false positives on partial reversions. If partial-reversion bugs emerge, that's a Phase 2 amendment with a more sophisticated diff-based check.

operator_other_side_considerations.argument_against_implementing: The bug occurred once and was self-recovered within 30 minutes by the decider's reconstruction logic. The pipeline already has multiple redundant protection layers (PROP-022 archive siblings, PROP-024 JSON validity gate, decider's pre-push integrity gate, agent-side claim discipline). Adding yet another guard increases the surface area for guard-on-guard bugs (e.g., an anti-reversion false-positive blocking a legitimate decider rescue) without proportionate benefit. Consider deferring until a second incident demonstrates this is a recurring failure pattern rather than a one-off.
operator_other_side_considerations.argument_for_implementing_anyway: This is the THIRD documented incident in the same bug class: (1) PROP-013-motivating event (2026-04-09 decider patch reversion), (2) ATT-2026-05-17T03:13 attention-inbox overwrite (8141f3ef recovery commit), (3) this incident. The pattern is established. The fix is small and additive — it cannot regress existing behavior because it only adds a SKIP path after the existing guard's pass path. The cost of waiting for a third recovery is more decider Opus cycles spent reconstructing state.
operator_other_side_considerations.compromise_option: Implement Option B in a 'shadow mode' first: the anti-reversion check fires and LOGS but does NOT skip — observe for 1 week, measure false-positive rate, then promote to enforcement. This mirrors the PROP-009 shadow→enforce promotion pattern that successfully de-risked the queue_id strict matching enforcement. Cost of shadow mode: extra log lines in monitor/integrity/workspace-sync-skips.jsonl with reason='anti-reversion-shadow', zero behavior change. If shadow shows zero false positives across the multi-writer files for ≥3 days, flip to enforce.

regression_check.before: smart_copy with mtime guard only. The 2026-05-17 incident (74d349ce reverting 71b045e2 + 65279e78) is reproducible: an agent writes stale snapshot to FUSE at mtime T2 > git commit time T1 → smart_copy pushes the stale content. Recovery requires manual decider reconstruction.
regression_check.after: smart_copy with mtime guard + anti-reversion check. The 2026-05-17 incident is NOT reproducible: even if mtime guard says 'FUSE is newer', the anti-reversion check computes the FUSE sha1 and walks `git log -n 20 -- expansion-tracker.json`. The stale FUSE content matches commit 09640e82 (or wherever the pre-modification state was last committed). smart_copy emits 'SKIP (anti-reversion; FUSE matches historic commit 09640e82): monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json' and does NOT push. State remains intact.
preserved_behaviors: Universal-pusher rescue path for decider 403 push failures — preserved (never-pushed content does not appear in git history).
JSON-validity gate (PROP-024) — runs before anti-reversion in Step 3, unaffected.
Mtime guard — runs FIRST as before; anti-reversion is an additional check, not a replacement.
Strand detection (PROP-013) — unchanged (operates on NEVER_PUSH path which short-circuits before the new check).

phase_1_shadow_mode.behavior: Anti-reversion check fires, LOGS to skip-log with reason 'anti-reversion-shadow', but the smart_copy still proceeds with the copy. Output: count of would-skips per run, persisted in monitor/integrity/workspace-sync-skips.jsonl. Tinker monitors and reports.
phase_1_shadow_mode.exit_criteria_promote: Zero false positives observed (i.e., every 'would-skip' event corresponds to an actual stale-FUSE incident). Promote to enforce.
phase_1_shadow_mode.exit_criteria_rollback: Any false positive observed (a 'would-skip' that, on inspection, would have blocked a legitimate FUSE→git update). Revise the algorithm before retry.

phase_2_enforce_mode.behavior: Anti-reversion check fires, LOGS with reason 'anti-reversion', and SKIPS the copy. No further action required from operator unless tinker reports sustained anti_reversion > 0 across many runs (which would indicate a writer-side bug worth fixing at source).

instrumentation.log_file: monitor/integrity/workspace-sync-skips.jsonl (existing) — anti-reversion entries get reason='anti-reversion' or 'anti-reversion-shadow' with the matched historic SHA in the raw line.
instrumentation.tinker_visibility: Tinker's existing soft-complaints grep over workspace-sync-runs/*.json catches anti_reversion > 0 entries. No new tinker logic required.

success_criteria.M1: Anti-reversion check implemented in monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md smart_copy helper, in shadow mode.
success_criteria.M2: Shadow mode runs for ≥3 days with zero false positives — promoted to enforce.
success_criteria.M3: At least one bug reproduction (synthetic test: write stale historic content to FUSE, verify smart_copy skips it) confirms the guard catches the 2026-05-17 failure mode.
success_criteria.M4: No regression in universal-pusher rescue path (decider 403 push failures continue to recover via workspace-sync within ~1 hour).
success_criteria.M5: monitor/integrity/workspace-sync-runs/run-*.json reports include skip_breakdown.anti_reversion count, observable via tinker's soft-complaints grep.

operator_preference_fit_table.minimize_workspace_sync_md_size_growth: +25 lines (~3% growth on a 200-line file). Acceptable.
operator_preference_fit_table.avoid_new_files: Yes — zero new files. Pure in-place edit.
operator_preference_fit_table.preserve_existing_NEVER_PUSH_invariants: Yes — no change to NEVER_PUSH list or is_never_push logic.
operator_preference_fit_table.operator_review_required_before_enforce: Yes — shadow mode first, operator-promoted to enforce per Phase 1/2 rollout.
operator_preference_fit_table.fits_existing_skip_log_infrastructure: Yes — reuses $SKIP_LOG, workspace-sync-skips.jsonl, and per-run report shape. New reason string only.

notes_for_operator: This PROP responds to operator directive HNOTE-DECIDER-WSYNC-EXP-TRACKER-20260517T133000 (issued 13:30Z; the decider authored it after reconstructing the correct state at 13:23Z).

I considered the directive's two suggested fixes (a) git-wins-on-newer semantics and (b) add to NEVER_PUSH. (a) is operationally equivalent to this PROP's content-hash anti-reversion check — both compare FUSE content against historic versions, but content-hash is faster and more precise than full content compare. (b) is contraindicated by workspace-sync.md's existing self-test for curmudgeon/tracker.json.

Shadow-mode-first is the conservative path. If operator prefers, the PROP can flip directly to enforce — the change is small enough that the regression risk is well-bounded and the bug fix is well-targeted.

Not bundled with PROP-044 (cross-run claim discipline) — that PROP fixes intra-Opus-analyst races; this PROP fixes inter-writer FUSE/git races. Different layers, different files, different teams of writers.

Not bundled with PROP-024 (JSON validity gate) — that PROP catches malformed JSON; this PROP catches valid-but-stale JSON. Both are pre-push integrity gates; PROP-045 is the semantic counterpart to PROP-024's structural check.

operator_decisions.rationale: Bug active and verified (workspace-sync reverted baby commit 13:00Z; FUSE expansion-tracker.json lagging HEAD per tinker 14:06 Finding 3). Anti-reversion logic mechanically sound (sha1-vs-history is unambiguous). Shadow-mode 3-7 days = riding bug another week for low marginal certainty. Operator accepts small false-positive risk in exchange for immediate protection.
